track.title: First Salary Money Plan
track.description: A practical route for protecting a first salary before investing pressure starts.

course.title: First Salary Money Plan: Core route
course.description: Salary buckets, emergency money, family support, and the first KES 10,000.

lessons.title: First salary mistakes to avoid
lessons.summary: Slow down lifestyle pressure, lending pressure, and investment pressure after payday.
lessons.introduction: The first salary can create many claims at once: rent, food, transport, family support, friends, debt, lifestyle, and investing pressure. The first goal is stability, not looking successful.
sections: Separate money before spending starts. A simple split protects essentials before emotions take over.
Do not judge progress by how quickly you open an investment account. Judge by whether next month is safer.
Keep a small learning amount if you want to explore products, but do not risk essentials.

scenario.title: Payday weekend
scenario.text: A new employee receives KES 42,000 and is immediately invited to contribute to a deal. They first mark rent, transport, food, debt, and emergency money.

lessons.mistake: Treating payday as permission to commit money before bills and obligations are visible.
lessons.action: Write your top five obligations before choosing any savings or investment route.
lessons.takeaway: First salary discipline protects your future choices.
lessons.source_note: Source note: This lesson is PesaRoute editorial guidance; verify product rules with official providers.

lessons.title: How to split your salary into buckets
lessons.summary: Use buckets for needs, buffer, obligations, learning, and flexible spending.
lessons.introduction: Salary buckets are not a strict formula. They are a way to see trade-offs before money disappears. The buckets should fit your rent, commute, family role, debt, and risk level.
sections: Needs come first: rent, food, transport, utilities, medicine, and unavoidable debt.
The buffer bucket protects the next emergency and helps reduce panic borrowing.
Learning money is a small amount used to understand a route without threatening essentials.

scenario.title: KES 10,000 after bills
scenario.text: After rent and transport, a first-jobber has KES 10,000. They split it into emergency money, family support, debt reduction, and a small learning range.

lessons.mistake: Copying another person's percentages without checking your own obligations.
lessons.action: Create four buckets for this month and give each one a KES range.
lessons.takeaway: A good split makes pressure visible before it becomes a rushed decision.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Use ranges if you do not want exact amounts in PesaRoute.

lessons.title: Emergency fund before risky investing
lessons.summary: Build a small liquid buffer before putting money into volatile or locked routes.
lessons.introduction: A risky or locked product can be useful for some goals, but it is not a replacement for cash you may need quickly. The emergency fund protects rent, food, transport, medical needs, and job disruption.
sections: Start with a realistic target such as one week or one month of core expenses.
Emergency money should have predictable access, even if the return is modest.
Risky investing without a buffer can force you to exit at the wrong time or borrow expensively.

scenario.title: Job delay example
scenario.text: A contract worker's pay is delayed by two weeks. Emergency money helps them avoid selling shares or taking expensive debt.

lessons.mistake: Using emergency money to chase a product because the return sounds exciting.
lessons.action: Write the minimum amount that would stop one emergency from becoming debt.
lessons.takeaway: A buffer makes future investing calmer.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify withdrawal timing with any provider before relying on a product for emergency access.

lessons.title: Managing family support and black tax
lessons.summary: Plan family support with dignity and boundaries instead of silent pressure.
lessons.introduction: Family support is real for many Kenyan first-jobbers. A plan helps you support people without destroying rent, transport, emergency money, or your own ability to grow.
sections: Make support visible in the budget instead of treating it as a surprise every week.
Use a range and a timing rule, such as one fixed support day each month.
Some requests are emergencies; others are important but can wait. Naming the difference reduces guilt-driven decisions.

scenario.title: Sibling school request
scenario.text: A first-jobber wants to help a sibling but also has rent due. They agree on a support range and date instead of sending money impulsively.

lessons.mistake: Saying yes to every request and then using debt to cover essentials.
lessons.action: Write one family-support boundary you can explain respectfully.
lessons.takeaway: Support is healthier when it is planned, visible, and sustainable.
lessons.source_note: Source note: PesaRoute does not judge family obligations; it helps make trade-offs visible.

lessons.title: What to do with your first KES 10,000
lessons.summary: Use the first KES 10,000 as a learning decision, not a performance contest.
lessons.introduction: The first KES 10,000 can teach a useful habit: compare needs, buffer, debt, and learning before choosing any route. The amount may be exact, rounded, ranged, or hidden.
sections: If essentials are not covered, the KES 10,000 may need to protect stability first.
If a small buffer exists, part of the money can be used to learn a low-complexity route.
The journal matters because it records why you acted or waited.

scenario.title: Two choices
scenario.text: A user can put the whole amount into a product or split it into emergency money and learning money. They compare how each choice affects next month.

lessons.mistake: Trying to look advanced before understanding fees, timing, and risk.
lessons.action: Save a journal entry explaining what the money is for and what would make you wait.
lessons.takeaway: A small amount can build a strong process.
lessons.source_note: Source note: Verify product-specific details with the provider or regulator before sending money.

quiz_questions.prompt: What is the first job of a first salary plan?
quiz_questions.explanation: A first salary plan should make essentials visible before optional decisions.

options: A way to separate purposes before spending
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A way to separate purposes before spending
quiz_questions.explanation: Buckets help you see needs, buffer, obligations, and learning money.

quiz_questions.prompt: Why build a buffer before volatile or locked routes?
options: To reduce panic exits and expensive borrowing
quiz_questions.correct_answer: To reduce panic exits and expensive borrowing
quiz_questions.explanation: A buffer protects basic needs when income or expenses change.

flashcards.front: What should first salary protect first?
flashcards.back: Essentials, obligations, and a small buffer.
flashcards.example: Rent and transport come before a product experiment.

flashcards.back: A small amount used to understand a route without risking essentials.
flashcards.example: KES 1,000 to compare a product route after needs are covered.

flashcards.back: To support family without creating avoidable debt or instability.
flashcards.example: A fixed monthly support range is clearer than random sends.

flashcards.back: Predictable access when a real need appears.
flashcards.example: Money for clinic or rent should not depend on market timing.

flashcards.back: To record the reason, risk, and reason to wait.
flashcards.example: A note can prevent acting from pressure.
